reason: State-level entries removed from federal TCKC Threat Tracker. Preserved here for potential reclassification into the BGHPN Storymap state-policy layer (FL/AL/MD/NY/MS and future states).

entries.n: Tennessee HB/SB Undocumented Student Exclusion Bill
entries.T: Tennessee Undocumented Student Exclusion Bill: Tennessee Legislation Would Bar Undocumented Children from Public Schools and Require Administrator Reporting
entries.D:
BILL: This measure, 119th Congress. Tennessee Undocumented Student Exclusion Bill: Tennessee Legislation Would Bar Undocumented Children from Public Schools and Require Administrator Reporting.

SPONSORS & COMMITTEES: Sponsor and committee referral details for this measure are tracked via Congress.gov. The bill has been referred to the relevant committee(s) of jurisdiction for consideration under standard congressional procedure.

KEY PROVISIONS: Tennessee Undocumented Student Exclusion Bill: Tennessee Legislation Would Bar Undocumented Children from Public Schools and Require Administrator Reporting. Involves DOE. Immigration enforcement actions disproportionately affect mixed-status families, DACA recipients, and long-established immigrant communities. According to the Migration Policy Institute, approximately 11 million undocumented individuals reside in the United States, with 4.4 million living with U.S. citizen family members. Enforcement escalation creates chilling effects on access to healthcare, education, and social services, even for lawfully present individuals, as documented by the Urban Institute and National Immigration Law Center.

LEGISLATIVE HISTORY: Introduced/tracked 2026-03-04. Advancing in Tennessee Legislature. Currently advancing through the legislative process at the state level. Tennessee legislation must pass both the House and Senate and be signed by the Governor to become law. The legislative process for this measure follows standard congressional procedure: introduction, committee referral, potential hearings and markup, floor consideration, and conference/reconciliation between chambers before presidential action. CRS Report RS20856 explains the legislative process in detail.

LEGAL MECHANISM: Congressional legislative authority under Article I, Section 1 of the U.S. Constitution. This measure proceeds through the standard legislative process: introduction, committee referral, floor debate, passage by both chambers, and presidential signature (or veto override) under Article I, Section 7.

EFFECTS: Tennessee Undocumented Student Exclusion Bill: Tennessee Legislation Would Bar Undocumented Children from Public Schools and Require Administrator Reporting poses a severe threat to the cultural heritage, civil rights, and community wellbeing of Immigrant/Refugee, Latiné, Indigenous/Tribal, All communities. This legislation threatens the rights, resources, and institutional support that these communities depend on. Immigration enforcement actions disproportionately affect mixed-status families, DACA recipients, and long-established immigrant communities. According to the Migration Policy Institute, approximately 11 million undocumented individuals reside in the United States, with 4.4 million living with U.S. citizen family members. Enforcement escalation creates chilling effects on access to healthcare, education, and social services, even for lawfully present individuals, as documented by the Urban Institute and National Immigration Law Center. Federal policy affecting tribal nations implicates the federal trust responsibility - the legal obligation of the United States to protect tribal sovereignty, lands, and resources established through treaties, statutes, and court decisions. The Supreme Court in Worcester v. Georgia (1832) and subsequent cases affirmed tribal sovereignty. CRS Report R44457 examines the federal-tribal relationship, and the Government Accountability Office has documented persistent funding shortfalls in Indian Health Service, Bureau of Indian Affairs, and tribal education programs (GAO-24-106290).

immigrant.people: Undocumented immigrant children and their families face direct, immediate, and severe harm from this legislation. An estimated 700,000 undocumented children attend U.S. public schools nationally, with tens of thousands enrolled in Tennessee's public school system. These children — the vast majority of whom have lived in the United States for years and know no other home — would face formal exclusion from the educational institutions that represent their primary pathway to civic participation, language acquisition, and economic integration. Many are U.S. citizens by birth whose undocumented parents would be exposed to reporting by administrators. Mixed-status families face the impossible choice between their children's education and their family's safety. The administrator-reporting provision transforms the school — an institution children are legally required by state law to attend — into a site of immigration enforcement, creating a profound and irresolvable trap. Parents who fear deportation and family separation will withdraw their children from schools before formal exclusion takes effect, producing a massive chilling effect that extends far beyond Tennessee. Unaccompanied minors, children who arrived as refugees, and youth in immigration proceedings face particular vulnerability. Latiné immigrant children constitute the largest affected demographic, but the bill would also affect undocumented children from Central America, the Caribbean, Africa, Asia, and Eastern Europe. The psychological trauma inflicted on children who are formally barred from school or who watch their parents be reported by their teachers is incalculable and well-documented in child welfare literature.
immigrant.places: Tennessee's public school districts with significant immigrant enrollment — including Metro Nashville Public Schools, Shelby County Schools in Memphis, Knox County Schools in Knoxville, and Hamilton County Schools in Chattanooga — would be ground zero for implementation. Nashville, which hosts one of the largest Kurdish communities in the United States as well as large Latiné, Somali, and Burmese immigrant populations, would see thousands of children displaced from school. Elementary, middle, and high schools in agricultural counties in West Tennessee and the Nashville Basin, where undocumented workers constitute a substantial portion of the workforce in meat processing, construction, and agriculture, would face mass withdrawals. Community centers, churches, and faith-based organizations serving immigrant communities in Tennessee — including Casa Azafrán in Nashville, Latino Memphis, and the Tennessee Immigrant and Refugee Rights Coalition offices — would be overwhelmed with families seeking guidance. Bilingual and English Language Learner (ELL) programs across the state, built over decades to serve immigrant students, would effectively collapse. Charter schools, Head Start centers, and early childhood education programs serving immigrant families would also face enrollment collapse as fear pervades immigrant communities regardless of individual children's documentation status.
immigrant.practices: The bill would devastate decades of established professional practice in Tennessee's public schools around immigrant student inclusion, English language acquisition, trauma-informed education, and family engagement. ELL teachers and bilingual educators — many of whom are themselves immigrants or children of immigrants — would be forced to choose between their professional obligation to educate students and legal mandates to report families. The 'sanctuary school' practices informally adopted by many Tennessee districts — including policies limiting cooperation with Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and ensuring that immigration status does not affect student enrollment — would be rendered illegal. Cultural liaison programs that schools have developed to build trust with immigrant communities, bring parents into school governance, and bridge communication across language barriers would be destroyed by the administrator-reporting mandate. The practice of home visits by school social workers and counselors, critical for reaching immigrant families who distrust formal institutions, would become legally fraught. Legal aid organizations and immigrant rights groups that have worked with school districts to inform immigrant families of their Plyler rights would be fighting a fundamentally changed legal landscape. The longstanding professional norm that schools are safe spaces where immigration status is irrelevant to a child's right to learn would be formally repudiated by state law.
immigrant.treasures: The cultural and educational resources at risk extend far beyond individual children's access to classrooms. Bilingual libraries and multilingual resource collections built by Tennessee school districts to serve immigrant students — including Spanish, Arabic, Somali, Kurdish Kurmanji, Burmese, and Vietnamese materials — would become stranded assets in schools emptied of the children they were assembled to serve. Community archives documenting the history and cultural contributions of Tennessee's immigrant communities, many housed in or connected to schools and educational institutions, face loss of the next generation of stewards. Oral history projects, cultural heritage documentation efforts, and intergenerational knowledge transfer programs run through schools — including those documenting the history of Nashville's Kurdish community, Memphis's significant Latiné labor history, and the refugee resettlement experiences of East African communities — would lose their institutional base. Artwork, murals, and cultural installations created by and for immigrant student communities in schools across Tennessee represent irreplaceable community cultural production that would be abandoned. Student journalism programs at schools with majority-immigrant enrollment that have documented community life, preserved community voices, and trained the next generation of cultural producers would collapse. The loss of an entire cohort of educated immigrant youth would also represent the loss of future cultural workers, translators, artists, historians, and community leaders who would otherwise have served as bridges between immigrant communities and broader American cultural life.

latine.people: Latiné communities bear the heaviest disproportionate impact of this legislation, as Latiné children constitute the largest segment of undocumented students in Tennessee and nationally. Mexican, Guatemalan, Honduran, Salvadoran, and other Central American and South American families who have built lives in Tennessee — many over decades — face the prospect of their children being formally expelled from schools where they may have enrolled, made friends, learned English, and developed a sense of belonging in American civic life. Latiné parents who work in Tennessee's meatpacking industry in cities like Shelby and Sevier counties, in construction across the Nashville metro, and in agriculture in rural West Tennessee face the loss of educational pathways for their children while continuing to contribute essential labor to the state economy. DACA recipients who are parents of undocumented younger siblings face particular family fragmentation. U.S.-born Latiné children whose parents are undocumented face the surveillance and reporting of their families by their own school administrators, poisoning the educational environment even for children with full legal status. Latiné advocacy organizations including the Tennessee Immigrant and Refugee Rights Coalition, the Tennessee Latino Community, and Conexión Américas have already been organizing in response to the legislation, but face an enormous resource disparity against the Heritage Foundation's national coordination infrastructure.
latine.places: The Latiné cultural geography of Tennessee — built over thirty years of immigration driven by NAFTA's economic dislocations, Central American violence, and Tennessee's labor market — is centered in communities that would be devastated by mass school exclusion. Nolensville Road in Nashville, known as the city's 'International Corridor,' hosts dozens of Latiné businesses, cultural organizations, restaurants, and community institutions whose economic vitality depends on a stable, rooted immigrant population. Schools along this corridor, including Antioch High School and various Metro Nashville elementary schools, have developed robust Latiné cultural programs, Día de los Muertos celebrations, Spanish-language parent engagement infrastructure, and ELL programs serving hundreds of families. In Shelby County, Latiné communities centered around South Memphis and Midtown have developed school-based cultural programs and parent organizations. In rural Tennessee counties including Bedford, Marshall, and Lawrence — home to large meat processing workforces — entire school populations would be hollowed out by mass withdrawals. Catholic parishes and evangelical churches that serve as cultural anchors for Latiné communities and that coordinate extensively with schools on family services would lose critical institutional partners.
latine.practices: Decades of Latiné community organizing to build culturally responsive education in Tennessee public schools would be legally undermined and practically destroyed by this legislation. Quinceañera preparation programs offered through some schools as cultural enrichment, Spanish-English dual language immersion programs built in districts like Metro Nashville over more than fifteen years, and Latiné history curriculum developed in response to community advocacy would all lose their student populations. The practice of informal cultural mediation by Latiné school staff — bilingual teachers, paraprofessionals, and cafeteria workers who serve as trusted bridges between immigrant families and school administration — would be ruptured by the legal mandate to report immigration status. Parent-teacher organizations in Latiné-majority schools that have served as models of immigrant civic participation would disintegrate under the reporting mandate. The tradition of Latiné families turning to schools as trusted community institutions — a practice that educational researchers document as central to immigrant integration — would be permanently poisoned. Latiné student organizations, MECHA chapters, and cultural clubs that have produced leadership pipelines in Tennessee schools would collapse with their membership base.
latine.treasures: The cultural production of Latiné students in Tennessee public schools represents an irreplaceable body of community expression and documentation. School-based murals painted by Latiné students depicting their communities' histories, migration journeys, and cultural heritage — including significant works in Nashville and Memphis schools — would be abandoned in institutions emptied of their creators. Student publications, including Spanish-English bilingual school newspapers and literary magazines produced at several Tennessee high schools, represent unique community archives that would cease publication. Bilingual children's book collections, piñata-making and traditional craft instruction programs, folklorico dance programs, and mariachi ensembles that have been established in Tennessee schools as both cultural preservation and student engagement tools would lose their institutional basis. Community memory projects in which Latiné students have interviewed grandparents and community elders about migration histories, labor experiences, and cultural traditions — producing recordings, transcripts, and multimedia archives — would lose their student researchers and the school partnerships that have housed these materials.

indigenous.people: This action affects the ancestral and contemporary territories of the Georgia Tribe of Eastern Cherokee, Lower Muskogee Creek Tribe, Muscogee, Cherokee, Hitchiti, Yuchi, Yamasee, and Guale of Georgia. The ancestral homelands of Georgia belong to peoples who were forcibly displaced, including Muscogee (central and southern Georgia was the heart of the Creek Confederacy; removed 183); Cherokee (northern Georgia including Dahlonega gold fields; removal forced by Georgia Guar); Yuchi (northern Georgia; removed with Creek); Guale (coastal Georgia/Sea Islands; destroyed by Spanish mission system and English rai). Their cultural resources — burial sites, ceremonial grounds, village sites, and sacred places — remain on these lands under federal management. This action affects the ancestral and contemporary territories of the Cherokee, Chickasaw, Muscogee, Shawnee, and Yuchi of Tennessee. This action affects the ancestral and contemporary territories of the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians, Lumbee Tribe, Haliwa-Saponi Tribe, Sappony, Occaneechi Band of the Saponi Nation, Waccamaw Siouan Tribe, Coharie Tribe, and Meherrin Indian Tribe of North Carolina. The ancestral homelands of North Carolina belong to peoples who were forcibly displaced, including Cherokee (western North Carolina was the heart of Cherokee country; most removed 1838, Eas); Tuscarora (eastern North Carolina; Tuscarora War 1711-15, most fled north to join Haudenosa). Their cultural resources — burial sites, ceremonial grounds, village sites, and sacred places — remain on these lands under federal management. This action affects the ancestral and contemporary territories of the Navajo Nation, Pueblo of Acoma, Pueblo of Cochiti, Pueblo of Isleta, Pueblo of Jemez, Pueblo of Laguna, Pueblo of Nambe, and Pueblo of Ohkay Owingeh of New Mexico. This action affects the ancestral and contemporary territories of the Catawba of North Carolina.
indigenous.places: Cultural resources in Georgia include: Ocmulgee Mounds National Historical Park — 17,000 years of continuous human habitation; Muscogee ancestral ceremonial center with earth lodge; Etowah Indian Mounds — Mississippian ceremonial center (1000–1550 CE), marble statues; Kolomoki Mounds — Woodland period (350–750 CE); Fort Frederica — colonial-era conflicts affecting Guale and Yamasee; Trail of Tears National Historic Trail — Cherokee removal routes through Georgia. Cultural resources in Tennessee include: Great Smoky Mountains National Park — Cherokee homeland (see North Carolina); Shiloh National Military Park — Mississippian mound complex predating Civil War by centuries; Stones River National Battlefield — on former Cherokee/Shawnee territory; Old Stone Fort State Archaeological Park — 2,000-year-old ceremonial enclosure; Pinson Mounds State Archaeological Park — Middle Woodland ceremonial center, Saul's Mound is second tallest in US. Cultural resources in Georgia include: New Echota State Historic Site — last capital of the Cherokee Nation before removal; Sapelo Island — Guale mission sites, also Gullah/Geechee heritage; Rock Eagle Effigy Mound — Woodland period quartz effigy. Cultural resources in North Carolina include: Blue Ridge Parkway — Cherokee ancestral territory, passes through Qualla Boundary; Carl Sandburg Home NHS — Cherokee territory context; Museum of the Cherokee Indian — Cherokee cultural institution, Cherokee, NC; Town Creek Indian Mound — Pee Dee culture ceremonial center (1150–1400 CE); Judaculla Rock — Cherokee petroglyph, largest in Southeast. Cultural resources in Tennessee include: Chucalissa Museum — Mississippian village, Memphis; Sequoyah Birthplace Museum — Vonore, Cherokee cultural institution; Red Clay State Historic Park — last Cherokee council ground before removal 1832–38. Cultural resources in New Mexico include: Chaco Culture National Historical Park — Ancestral Puebloan great houses and roads, astronomical alignments; all 20 pueblos claim cultural affiliation; Bandelier National Monument — Ancestral Puebloan cliff dwellings, Cochiti/San Ildefonso ancestral territory; Gila Cliff Dwellings — Mogollon culture, Apache territory; Aztec Ruins National Monument — Ancestral Puebloan great house, Chaco outlier; Petroglyph National Monument — 24,000 petroglyphs, Pueblo sacred landscape.
indigenous.practices: Indigenous cultural practices and concepts relevant to North Carolina: Cherokee syllabary — writing system invented by Sequoyah (ᏍᏏᏉᏯ), one of few independently created writing systems in history; Qualla Boundary — Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians trust land, not technically a reservation; Gadugi — Cherokee cooperative labor tradition. Indigenous cultural practices and concepts relevant to New Mexico: Pueblo worldview — emergence from underworld, connection of all living things through sacred landscape; Kiva — sacred ceremonial chamber, heart of Pueblo spiritual practice; Corn/maize — Pueblo agricultural-spiritual foundation; multiple colored corn varieties with ceremonial significance; All Pueblo Council — governing body uniting 20 pueblos, operating since at least 1598 (Pueblo Revolt organization).
indigenous.treasures: Indigenous cultural treasures of this region include Stomp Dance regalia, rivercane baskets, shell gorgets, chunkey stones, copper plates from Mississippian era. Indigenous cultural treasures of this region include Stomp Dance regalia, rivercane baskets, shell gorgets, chunkey stones, copper plates from Mississippian era. Indigenous cultural treasures of this region include Katsina/Kachina carvings, Navajo weavings, Pueblo pottery, turquoise and silver jewelry, sand paintings.

africanDescendant.people: African-descendant communities in Arizona include Phoenix South Mountain/Laveen; Tucson African American community; Maryvale, Phoenix; Mesa/Chandler. Afro-Indigenous communities in this region include: Buffalo Soldiers (Black cavalry stationed at Arizona forts (Fort Huachuca); fought in Apache Wars; complex legacy of s); Black Seminoles (Seminole Negro Indian Scouts) (historically passed through Arizona territory); Afro-Diné (Navajo) (individuals with both African and Navajo ancestry). African-descendant communities in Georgia include Atlanta: Auburn Avenue ("Sweet Auburn"); Savannah; Augusta; Macon; Albany. Afro-Indigenous communities in this region include: Freedmen of the Cherokee Nation (descendants of people enslaved by Cherokee in Georgia before removal; many remained in Georgia); Freedmen of the Creek Nation (descendants of people enslaved by Muscogee Creek); Gullah/Geechee-Indigenous connections (documented intermarriage between Sea Islands communities and coastal Indigenous peoples). African-descendant communities in North Carolina include Durham; Charlotte; Raleigh; Greensboro; Winston-Salem. African-descendant communities in Tennessee include Memphis; Nashville; Knoxville; Chattanooga. African-descendant communities in Florida include Miami: Liberty City; Jacksonville; Tampa; Orlando; Tallahassee. Afro-Indigenous communities in this region include: Black Seminoles (Seminole Maroons) (African-descended people who escaped enslavement and allied with Seminole; fought alongside Seminole); Afro-Seminole Creole language (unique English-based creole still spoken by some descendants); Gullah/Geechee communities of northeast Florida (Amelia Island, Fort George Island; extension of Sea Islands culture). African-descendant communities in Georgia include Sapelo Island; St. Simons Island/Sea Islands; Darien; Columbus Black community; African immigrant communities. African-descendant communities in New Mexico include Albuquerque; Las Cruces, Roswell; Blackdom (Chaves County). Afro-Indigenous communities in this region include: Afro-Indigenous New Mexicans (small but documented community of people with African and Pueblo or Navajo ancestry; some trace to S); Estevanico/Esteban (1539) (Moroccan/African explorer who was the first non-Indigenous person to enter the Pueblo world; critica). African-descendant communities in New York include New York City; Harlem; Bedford-Stuyvesant (Brooklyn); Crown Heights (Brooklyn); Flatbush/East Flatbush (Brooklyn). Afro-Indigenous communities in this region include: Afro-Shinnecock (documented African-Shinnecock intermarriage on Long Island since 1700s; some Shinnecock Nation membe); Black Haudenosaunee (historical presence of African-descended people in Haudenosaunee communities; some escaped slavery t). African-descendant communities in North Carolina include Wilmington; Princeville. African-descendant communities in Oregon include Portland; Vanport.
africanDescendant.places: African-descendant cultural resources in Arizona: Fort Huachuca — Buffalo Soldiers history, only active fort from Indian Wars era; George Washington Carver Museum and Cultural Center (Phoenix); Dunbar School (Tucson) — segregated-era school, now community center; Phoenix African American community oral history projects. African-descendant cultural resources in Georgia: Martin Luther King Jr. National Historical Park (Atlanta) — birth home, Ebenezer Baptist Church, crypt; Sweet Auburn Historic District (Atlanta) — "richest Negro street in the world" (Forbes 1956); SCLC headquarters; Ebenezer Baptist Church (Atlanta) — MLK Sr. and Jr. pastored; National Historic Landmark; Atlanta University Center — Morehouse College, Spelman College, Clark Atlanta University, Morris Brown College; largest consortium of HBCUs in the world; National Center for Civil and Human Rights (Atlanta). African-descendant cultural resources in North Carolina: International Civil Rights Center & Museum (Greensboro) — Woolworth lunch counter sit-in site (1960); Hayti Heritage Center (Durham) — remains of "Black Wall Street of the South"; North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company (Durham) — largest Black-owned business in US for decades; Shaw University (Raleigh) — SNCC was founded here in 1960; Bennett College (Greensboro) — HBCU for women. African-descendant cultural resources in Tennessee: National Civil Rights Museum (Memphis) — Lorraine Motel, where MLK was assassinated April 4, 1968; Beale Street Historic District (Memphis) — "Home of the Blues"; W.C. Handy Park; Stax Museum of American Soul Music (Memphis) — Soulsville USA; Sun Studio (Memphis) — recorded early blues and rock; interracial musical exchange; Fisk University (Nashville) — HBCU, Jubilee Hall (oldest permanent structure for higher education of Black students), Aaron Douglas murals. African-descendant cultural resources in Florida: Eatonville Historic District — first Black incorporated town in US (1887); Zora Neale Hurston's birthplace; Zora Neale Hurston National Museum of Fine Arts (Eatonville); Fort Mose Historic State Park (St. Augustine) — first legally sanctioned free Black settlement in North America (1738), sanctuary for escaped enslaved persons from Carolina colonies; American Beach (Amelia Island) — Black beach community founded 1935 during Jim Crow; A.L. Lewis, Florida's first Black millionaire; FAMU campus (Tallahassee) — Florida A&M University, HBCU heritage. African-descendant cultural resources in Georgia: APEX Museum (Atlanta) — African American Panoramic Experience; First African Baptist Church (Savannah) — one of oldest Black churches in North America (1773); breathing holes in floor where enslaved people hid in escape tunnel; Sapelo Island Hog Hammock (Gullah/Geechee) — NPS study, National Register district; Pin Point Heritage Museum (Savannah) — Gullah/Geechee oyster community, Justice Clarence Thomas's hometown; Tubman Museum (Macon) — largest museum in Southeast dedicated to African American history. African-descendant cultural resources in New Mexico: Blackdom historic site (near Dexter, NM) — archaeological remains of all-Black town; Fort Bayard — Buffalo Soldiers, 9th Cavalry. African-descendant cultural resources in New York: Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture (Harlem, NYPL) — world's leading research facility devoted to Black history and culture; Arturo Alfonso Schomburg's original collection; National Museum of African American History (proposed Harlem extension); Studio Museum in Harlem — premier museum of Black art; Apollo Theater (Harlem) — "the Palace" of Black entertainment since 1934; African Burial Ground National Monument (Lower Manhattan) — 15,000+ enslaved and free Africans buried 1690s–1790s; discovered 1991; most important urban archaeological project in US history. African-descendant cultural resources in North Carolina: NC A&T State University (Greensboro) — Greensboro Four students, largest HBCU in NC; 1898 Wilmington Coup memorial — only successful violent overthrow of elected US government; mob destroyed Black newspaper Daily Record; Princeville (Edgecombe County) — oldest Black-incorporated town in America; Stagville Plantation (Durham) — one of largest plantations in the South; extensive enslaved persons' community documentation. African-descendant cultural resources in Oregon: Oregon Black Pioneers — historical organization documenting Black Oregon history; Vanport Mosaic project — documenting the destroyed city; Billy Webb Elks Lodge (Portland) — historic Black social club; Golden West Hotel (Portland) — one of few hotels that would serve Black travelers. African-descendant cultural resources in Tennessee: Meharry Medical College (Nashville) — first medical school for Black Americans in the South (1876); Tennessee State University (Nashville) — HBCU; Wilma Rudolph, Oprah Winfrey attended; Alex Haley House Museum (Henning) — Roots author; Orange Mound community (Memphis) — first neighborhood in US built by and for Black Americans; Slave Haven Underground Railroad Museum / Burkle Estate (Memphis). African-derived spiritual and cultural sites in the US include: Oyotunji African Village (Sheldon, SC), the only Yoruba village in North America, founded 1970 by Oba Adefunmi I, maintaining Ogun, Oshun, Shango, and Obatala shrines and traditional Yoruba governance; Congo Square (New Orleans), where enslaved Africans gathered on Sundays to drum, dance, and maintain West African cultural traditions — the birthplace of American musical culture; the African Burial Ground National Monument (NYC), where 15,000+ enslaved and free Africans were buried with African spiritual objects (1690s–1790s); the Church of Lukumi Babalu Aye (Hialeah, FL), which won the 1993 Supreme Court case establishing Santería/Lukumí animal sacrifice as protected religious practice; Gullah/Geechee praise houses on the Sea Islands (SC/GA), small wooden buildings where ring shout — the most direct West African worship survival in the Americas — is still practiced; and the canopied bottle tree tradition visible across the rural South (Mississippi, Alabama, South Carolina), a Kongo/BaKongo cosmological practice of trapping harmful spirits in blue bottles. These sites connect to the African continent's heritage sites: Goree Island/Door of No Return (Senegal), Cape Coast Castle and Elmina Castle (Ghana), Ouidah (Benin), and the Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove (Nigeria, UNESCO World Heritage Site) — the spiritual source of Yoruba/Orisha traditions practiced in the diaspora. The Mississippi Delta — the alluvial floodplain between Memphis and Vicksburg — is the most culturally significant African-descendant region in the US. Specific communities and cultural landmarks: Clarksdale (crossroads of Highways 61 and 49; Muddy Waters, Son House, John Lee Hooker connections); Indianola (B.B. King's hometown); Ruleville (Fannie Lou Hamer's home — "I'm sick and tired of being sick and tired"); Money (Bryant's Grocery, where 14-year-old Emmett Till was accused, 1955); Mound Bayou (all-Black town founded 1887, "Jewel of the Delta"); Dockery Plantation (birthplace of the Delta Blues — Charley Patton, Howlin' Wolf, Roebuck Staples); Parchman Farm/Mississippi State Penitentiary (prison blues documented by John and Alan Lomax; Freedom Riders imprisoned 1961); Rolling Fork (Muddy Waters's birthplace); Greenwood (SNCC voter registration headquarters 1962–64); and the Hill Country Blues territory of Tate/Panola County (R.L. Burnside, Junior Kimbrough, Fred McDowell). The Delta's cultural practices include field hollers, juke joints, fife and drum (direct West African musical survival), river baptisms, Decoration Day/graveyard cleaning (ancestor veneration), and the hot tamale tradition (Afro-Latino culinary convergence).
africanDescendant.practices: Cultural practices in Arizona: Buffalo Soldier heritage commemorations — annual events at Fort Huachuca; East African immigrant cultural maintenance — Ethiopian/Eritrean coffee ceremonies, Somali community gatherings, Islamic religious practices adapted to desert Southwest. Cultural practices in Georgia: Gullah/Geechee culture — Sea Islands of Georgia coast; language, ring shout, sweetgrass basketry, net-making, indigo dyeing, rice cultivation knowledge from Sierra Leone/Senegambia; threatened by resort development and sea-level rise; Ring shout — West African–derived circular religious dance, preserved most fully in McIntosh County, Georgia; McIntosh County Shouters are the oldest known practitioners; Atlanta hip-hop — trap music originated in Atlanta (Zone 6, Bankhead); continuation of African-descendant musical innovation; global cultural export; Atlanta Black arts scene — Auburn Avenue, Sweet Auburn; historically centered Black literary, visual, and performing arts. Cultural practices in North Carolina: Piedmont blues — Blind Boy Fuller, Reverend Gary Davis; lighter, ragtime-influenced acoustic blues distinct from Delta; HBCU homecoming traditions — NC A&T, NC Central, Shaw, Johnson C. Smith; marching band competitions, step shows, tailgating; Jabberwock step competitions — Black sorority step show tradition, strong in NC; Carolina shag (dance) — interracial dance tradition from Carolina beaches; beach music. Cultural practices in Tennessee: Memphis Blues — W.C. Handy documented and published Delta blues; Beale Street as Black entertainment epicenter; foundation of rock and roll; Memphis soul — Stax Records (Otis Redding, Sam & Dave, Booker T. & the MGs, Isaac Hayes), Hi Records (Al Green); raw, gritty soul distinct from Motown polish; Jubilee Singers tradition — Fisk Jubilee Singers' 1871 tour saved Fisk University and introduced Negro spirituals to the world; sacred choral tradition continues; Memphis barbecue — Black pitmaster tradition; dry rub, pulled pork shoulder; Cozy Corner, Payne's, A&R. Cultural practices in Florida: Haitian Vodou (Voudou/Voodoo) — spiritual tradition of profound historical significance; practitioners in Little Haiti maintain ceremonies, peristyles, and botanical knowledge; Vodou was the spiritual foundation of the Haitian Revolution (1791–1804), the only successful slave revolt in history; Haitian Rara — street music processions, particularly during Lent/Easter; bamboo trumpets, drums, call-and-response; practiced in Little Haiti; Haitian Compas/Konpa music — popular dance music tradition maintained in diaspora; Junkanoo (Key West/Bahamian) — Afro-Bahamian masquerade tradition with roots in West African masquerade; performed at Christmas/New Year. Cultural practices in Georgia: Savannah Second African Baptist Church traditions — one of the oldest Black congregations in North America; Geechee fishing traditions — cast-net fishing, crabbing, oyster harvesting using techniques with West African origins. Cultural practices in New Mexico: Buffalo Soldier heritage in New Mexico — 9th and 10th Cavalry stationed at Fort Bayard, Fort Stanton, Fort Selden. Cultural practices in New York: Harlem Renaissance — 1920s–30s explosion of Black art, literature, music, and thought; Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Claude McKay, Aaron Douglas, Augusta Savage, Duke Ellington; most influential Black cultural movement in American history; Bebop jazz — born at Minton's Playhouse and Monroe's Uptown House in Harlem; Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, Thelonious Monk; revolution in American music; West Indian Day Parade/Carnival (Labor Day, Eastern Parkway) — largest Caribbean carnival in North America; 1–3 million participants; soca, calypso, steel pan, mas (masquerade); rooted in Trinidad Carnival tradition; Brooklyn dancehall culture — Jamaican dancehall and reggae maintained in Caribbean community. Cultural practices in Oregon: Oregon exclusion law legacy — Oregon was only state admitted with an exclusion law barring Black residents (1857); shapes current demographics and racial dynamics; Albina community preservation efforts — against ongoing gentrification. Cultural practices in Tennessee: Nashville sit-in tradition — John Lewis, Diane Nash led lunch counter sit-ins 1960; Nashville Student Movement.
africanDescendant.treasures: African-descendant cultural treasures of this region include Black Panther Party archives and ephemera, Freedmen's town artifacts, Buffalo Soldiers military artifacts, Black cowboy and rodeo heritage materials, Juneteenth historical documentation. African-descendant cultural treasures of this region include Gullah/Geechee sweetgrass baskets, quilts (Gee's Bend tradition), wrought ironwork (West African metalworking), indigo-dyed textiles, enslaved persons' pottery (Edgefield/Colonoware). African-descendant cultural treasures of this region include Gullah/Geechee sweetgrass baskets, quilts (Gee's Bend tradition), wrought ironwork (West African metalworking), indigo-dyed textiles, enslaved persons' pottery (Edgefield/Colonoware). African-descendant cultural treasures of this region include Gullah/Geechee sweetgrass baskets, quilts (Gee's Bend tradition), wrought ironwork (West African metalworking), indigo-dyed textiles, enslaved persons' pottery (Edgefield/Colonoware). African-descendant cultural treasures of this region include shotgun house architecture. African-descendant cultural treasures of this region include shotgun house architecture. African-descendant cultural treasures of this region include African Burial Ground artifacts, Schomburg Center collections, AME church institutional records, Harlem Renaissance artworks and manuscripts, Underground Railroad safe house documentation. African-descendant cultural treasures of this region include shotgun house architecture. African-descendant cultural treasures of this region include shotgun house architecture.

entries.n: Arizona 2026 Midterm Election Administration — Maricopa County
entries.T: Arizona 2026: Maricopa County Election Administration Under Election Skeptic Justin Heap Raises Voting Rights Concerns Ahead of Competitive Midterms
entries.S: Active — 2026 Arizona midterm elections underway with election skeptic Justin Heap overseeing Maricopa County elections. Multiple competitive U.S. House races and statewide Democratic-held seats in play.
entries.D:
DOMESTIC POLICY: Arizona's 2026 midterm election cycle has drawn national attention to Maricopa County, the state's largest county and one of the most watched election jurisdictions in the United States. Republican Justin Heap, who has been characterized as an election skeptic, is overseeing elections in the Phoenix metropolitan area — a jurisdiction that became ground zero for election denial and conspiracy theories following the 2020 and 2022 election cycles. Heap's position as the chief election administrator in Maricopa County is particularly consequential given the county's size and demographic diversity, encompassing millions of registered voters including large Latino, Native American, African American, and Asian American communities whose electoral participation has historically been contested and litigated.

CONTEXT AND BACKGROUND: Maricopa County's election administration office has been the subject of extensive audits, lawsuits, legislative investigations, and partisan attacks since 2020. The county's election system was subjected to a widely discredited partisan audit in 2021, and its officials faced threats and harassment from election deniers. The elevation of an election skeptic to oversee this office in 2026 represents a significant shift in how the county will approach ballot counting, voter roll maintenance, polling place access, and adjudication of challenged ballots. Election skeptics have historically supported restrictive interpretations of voter eligibility, aggressive voter roll purges, and administrative processes that can delay or complicate vote counting — all of which disproportionately affect communities of color.

ELECTORAL STAKES: Arizona is expected to feature at least two competitive U.S. House races in November 2026. Democrats are simultaneously defending statewide offices including governor, attorney general, and secretary of state — a trifecta of positions that together control how state laws are interpreted, how elections are administered statewide, and how legal challenges to election results are prosecuted. The combination of a skeptic-led Maricopa County election office and hotly contested statewide races creates conditions under which administrative decisions — about ballot curing procedures, drop box availability, multilingual ballot access, and provisional ballot adjudication — could directly affect electoral outcomes.

VOTING RIGHTS IMPLICATIONS: Maricopa County has been a flashpoint for voting rights litigation affecting Latino and Native American voters in particular. The county's size means that administrative decisions about polling place locations, hours, equipment deployment, and voter assistance affect hundreds of thousands of voters. Native American communities in surrounding areas have faced longstanding barriers to voting including lack of residential addresses, limited polling locations, and inadequate language assistance. Latino voters in Phoenix and its suburbs have faced ballot intimidation and misinformation campaigns in recent cycles. The presence of an election skeptic overseeing administration adds an additional layer of risk that these communities' votes may face heightened scrutiny or administrative obstacles.

CULTURAL RESOURCE DIMENSION: Democratic participation and political representation are foundational to the ability of minority communities to protect their cultural resources, languages, sacred sites, and community institutions through the political process. When election administration is compromised or perceived as partisan, communities of color — who disproportionately rely on federal and state programs for cultural preservation, language access, tribal sovereignty protections, and anti-discrimination enforcement — face compounded threats. An election skeptic overseeing the largest county in a swing state represents a systemic threat to the representational power of these communities.

indigenous.people: Native American voters in the greater Phoenix metropolitan area and on reservations within or near Maricopa County face particular vulnerability under an election administration led by someone characterized as an election skeptic. Arizona's tribal nations — including the Tohono O'odham, Navajo, Gila River Indian Community, Salt River Pima-Maricopa Indian Community, Ak-Chin Indian Community, and Fort McDowell Yavapai Nation — have fought hard-won battles for voting access over decades, including landmark litigation establishing rights to language assistance, extended registration deadlines, and accessible polling locations. An administration skeptical of election integrity could reverse administrative accommodations that make voting accessible to Native communities, including mobile voting units, extended early voting on reservations, and Navajo and other Indigenous language ballot assistance. The political representation of tribal nations at the state and federal level directly affects tribal sovereignty, federal Indian law enforcement, sacred site protections under NHPA and RFRA, water rights, and federal trust responsibilities. Erosion of Native voting power in Maricopa County could shift legislative and executive decisions away from tribal interests at a moment when federal cultural resource protections for tribes are already under threat.
indigenous.places: Salt River Pima-Maricopa Indian Community and Gila River Indian Community lands lie within or directly adjacent to Maricopa County, making their residents directly subject to county election administration decisions. Polling locations on and near reservation boundaries, early voting sites accessible to tribal members, and drop box locations near tribal community centers are all subject to administrative decisions by the county recorder's office. The Heard Museum in Phoenix, which serves as a cultural anchor for urban Native communities in the Phoenix metro area, draws visitors from tribal communities whose political voice shapes funding and policy for Indigenous cultural institutions. Legislative decisions affecting the Arizona State Museum and tribal historic preservation programs flow from elections administered in Maricopa County. Federal Indian education funding, which supports Native language preservation programs at schools serving Maricopa-area tribal members, is similarly linked to federal electoral outcomes shaped by Arizona's competitive congressional races.
indigenous.practices: Tribal nations' practice of political self-determination — including advocacy before the Arizona Legislature and Congress on issues of water rights, gaming compacts, sacred site protection, and cultural repatriation — depends fundamentally on the ability of tribal members to vote and have their votes counted fairly. Election skepticism in Maricopa County administration could manifest in practices such as aggressive challenges to voter registrations using post office box addresses common on reservations, reduced polling locations requiring longer travel for rural tribal voters, inadequate language assistance for non-English-speaking tribal elders, and slower adjudication of provisional ballots cast by tribal members. The cultural practice of voting itself has become increasingly central to tribal civic identity, particularly following major Native voting rights victories in Arizona. Administrative barriers or post-election challenges that undermine confidence in the process erode this civic cultural practice. Tribal historic preservation offices and SHPOs depend on state and federal officials sympathetic to consultation requirements under Section 106 of the NHPA — officials whose election is shaped by Maricopa County results.
indigenous.treasures: The cultural treasures at risk from compromised election administration in Maricopa County are those that depend on political representation for their protection. Sacred sites throughout Arizona, including those subject to ongoing federal infrastructure projects and land transfers, are protected or endangered depending on who controls the governor's office, congressional delegation, and federal agencies — all shaped by Arizona electoral outcomes. Tribal archives held by nations in the Phoenix area, including oral history collections, ceremonial records, and cultural patrimony documentation, depend on federal and state funding streams influenced by Arizona's congressional delegation. NAGPRA implementation, which governs repatriation of ancestral remains and sacred objects held by Arizona museums and universities, depends on federal agency posture shaped by electoral politics. The Pueblo Grande Museum, a city-owned archaeological site in Phoenix containing Hohokam cultural resources, is subject to city budget decisions influenced by Maricopa County electoral dynamics.

latine.people: Latino voters constitute a major demographic bloc in Maricopa County, concentrated in Phoenix neighborhoods including Maryvale, South Mountain, and the West Valley, as well as in cities like Avondale, Goodyear, Glendale, and Mesa. Arizona's Latino community has faced repeated voting rights challenges including ballot intimidation by armed groups at drop boxes in the 2022 cycle, Spanish-language misinformation campaigns, and concerns about voter roll purges targeting naturalized citizens. An election skeptic overseeing Maricopa County administration raises the risk that administrative discretion will be exercised in ways that disproportionately burden Latino voters — through decisions about Spanish-language ballot access, voter registration maintenance, polling place deployment in majority-Latino precincts, and the processing of mail ballots. Latino voters in Arizona have disproportionately high rates of mail ballot use and provisional ballot casting, both of which are subject to significant administrative discretion. The political representation of Arizona's Latino community at the state and federal level directly affects cultural resource issues including Spanish-language government services, bilingual education, immigration enforcement affecting mixed-status families, and federal arts and cultural programs serving Latino communities.
latine.places: Phoenix's predominantly Latino neighborhoods are served by polling locations, early voting sites, and ballot drop boxes whose placement and hours are controlled by Maricopa County election administration. The Maryvale neighborhood, one of the most densely Latino communities in the Phoenix metro area, has historically had fewer polling resources relative to its population. The Cesar Chavez neighborhood and South Phoenix communities, with significant Mexican American and Indigenous Mexican populations, similarly depend on county decisions about resource allocation. The Guadalupe community, a historically Yaqui and Mexican American enclave within Maricopa County, sits at the intersection of Indigenous and Latino cultural communities whose political representation shapes protections for their heritage. Cultural institutions including Barrio Youth Project, Chicanos Por La Causa, and the Heard Museum's programs serving Latino communities all depend on political representation shaped by Maricopa County elections. Spanish-language media outlets and community organizations in Phoenix that provide voter education face heightened challenges when county administration is led by officials skeptical of established election processes.
latine.practices: The practice of civic participation within Arizona's Latino community — developed over generations of civil rights organizing, labor union activism through the United Farm Workers, and community institution-building — is directly threatened when election administration is perceived as or actually operates in a biased manner. Spanish-language voter assistance, required under Section 203 of the Voting Rights Act given Arizona's covered language minority populations, depends on good-faith county implementation of federal requirements. Ballots challenged or rejected at higher rates in Latino precincts suppress not only individual votes but the communal practice of electoral participation. The tradition of community voter registration drives, naturalization celebration voting, and multi-generational family civic participation that characterizes many Arizona Latino communities is chilled by an environment of election skepticism that treats minority voters' ballots as suspect. Cultural celebrations like Día de los Muertos, which in recent years have incorporated voter registration and civic participation components in Phoenix, take on diminished meaning when the process itself is under challenge.
latine.treasures: Arizona's Latino cultural heritage — including Spanish colonial archives at the Arizona State Library, Mexican American oral history collections at Arizona State University, and the cultural patrimony of Tohono O'odham, Yaqui, and other Indigenous nations with strong ties to Mexican American communities — is protected or endangered depending on who controls state and federal offices whose elections run through Maricopa County. The Spanish-language archives of Arizona newspapers like La Voz and the Arizona Republic's Spanish editions document community history that depends on cultural funding programs shaped by political representation. Public murals in Phoenix's Latino neighborhoods, some commissioned through city arts programs and others community-created, are subject to preservation or destruction based on city council and mayoral elections administered by county officials. The cultural programming of the Mesa Arts Center, Phoenix Art Museum's Latino collections, and Tempe's cultural institutions are budget-dependent on political outcomes in Maricopa County races.

africanDescendant.people: Black voters in Maricopa County, concentrated in communities including South Phoenix, Laveen, and portions of Scottsdale and Tempe, face the familiar pattern of election administration decisions that nominally apply equally but produce racially disparate impacts. Arizona's Black community, while smaller in absolute numbers than Latino or Native populations, has been significantly affected by Arizona's strict voter ID laws, aggressive voter roll maintenance practices, and the loss of key Voting Rights Act preclearance protections after Shelby County v. Holder (2013). An election skeptic overseeing Maricopa County could double down on administrative practices — including rejecting ballots with minor technical deficiencies, reducing curing opportunities, or challenging voter registrations — that disproportionately affect Black voters. The 2026 competitive races for governor, attorney general, and secretary of state create conditions in which every vote matters, and administrative discretion in a skeptic-led office could tip outcomes. Black political representation shapes federal funding for HBCUs, the future of the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture and its relationships with Arizona African American community organizations, and civil rights enforcement affecting Black Arizonans.
africanDescendant.places: South Phoenix, historically a predominantly Black community that has faced urban renewal displacement and now gentrification pressures, is a key site where voting access intersects with community survival. The George Washington Carver Museum and Cultural Center in Phoenix, which documents African American history in Arizona, exists in a political environment shaped by county and state elections. The Phoenix metro area's Black churches, which have historically served as voter registration and mobilization sites, face a more challenging environment when county election administration is led by officials skeptical of established voting processes. Arizona State University's Institute for Humanities Research and its programs documenting Black Arizona history are dependent on state budget decisions shaped by statewide elections administered through Maricopa County. The Burton Barr Central Library in Phoenix, which houses significant African American history collections, is a city institution whose funding is linked to local electoral dynamics.
africanDescendant.practices: The Black church tradition of civic mobilization — 'Souls to the Polls' drives, voter education from pulpits, and collective voting as a community practice — faces a hostile administrative environment when county election leadership is skeptical of the processes that make mass voting possible. Sunday early voting, which facilitates 'Souls to the Polls' in jurisdictions that offer it, is subject to administrative decisions by county election offices. The cultural practice of collective political action that has characterized African American civic life since Reconstruction is both symbolically and practically threatened by election administration that treats minority voting as suspect. Community organizations including the Phoenix chapter of the NAACP, the Phoenix Urban League, and local Black Lives Matter affiliates have invested in voter registration and civic education programs that depend on a functioning, accessible election system.
africanDescendant.treasures: African American cultural archives in Arizona — including collections at the Arizona African American Museum, oral histories held by community organizations, and the records of civil rights organizations that fought for voting rights in the state — document a history of struggle for political inclusion that is directly implicated when that inclusion is threatened by election skeptic administration. The cultural memory of what it cost to win voting rights makes administrative erosion of those rights particularly painful and significant as a cultural harm. Church records, community newspapers, and organizational archives of Black Phoenix institutions represent irreplaceable documentation of African American life in the Southwest that depends on community political power to ensure its preservation and public recognition.

allCommunities.people: All Arizonans — regardless of race, ethnicity, or cultural background — are affected by the quality and integrity of election administration in Maricopa County, the state's largest and most electorally consequential jurisdiction. When the official overseeing elections is characterized as an election skeptic, confidence in the process is eroded across the political spectrum, and the downstream effects on governance, cultural policy, and public investment affect everyone. The competitive races for governor, attorney general, and secretary of state mean that Maricopa County's administration of the 2026 midterms will shape Arizona's political direction for at least the next four years — determining who controls state cultural agencies, state arts funding, public university governance, and enforcement of civil rights and environmental laws that protect community resources. Election skepticism at the county level also creates conditions for post-election litigation and administrative disputes that delay the seating of elected officials and create governance uncertainty affecting all public programs.
allCommunities.places: Every public institution in Arizona whose budget, governance, or legal framework is determined by state and federal elected officials is indirectly affected by the integrity of Maricopa County's election administration. The Arizona State Library, Archives and Public Records; the Arizona Historical Society; the Arizona Commission on the Arts; the state's public universities; the Arizona Department of Environmental Quality; and the Arizona Department of Transportation all operate under political leadership whose composition is shaped by Maricopa County electoral outcomes. National monuments and federal lands in Arizona — including Agua Fria National Monument, Casa Grande Ruins National Monument, and Tonto National Monument — are subject to federal policy shaped by Arizona's congressional delegation, whose electoral fate runs through Maricopa County competitive races.
allCommunities.practices: Democratic deliberation, civic trust, and participatory governance — practices foundational to a functioning pluralistic society — are damaged when election administration is led by officials who have expressed skepticism about the legitimacy of established electoral processes. The practice of accepting election results, supporting peaceful transitions of power, and engaging in good-faith civic participation is undermined by administrative environments that treat routine election processes as suspect. Cultural institutions that depend on public trust and broad community engagement — museums, libraries, public arts programs — are weakened in environments of civic distrust that election skepticism fosters.
allCommunities.treasures: The democratic process itself is a civic treasure — the accumulated institutional knowledge, legal precedent, administrative practice, and community trust that makes Arizona's elections function is a resource built over generations. When that process is overseen by officials skeptical of its integrity, the institutional knowledge and nonpartisan administrative culture that protects elections from abuse are at risk. Public records of election administration, voter rolls, and ballot adjudication decisions — which form a historical archive of democratic participation — are subject to manipulation or mismanagement under skeptic leadership. The historical record of Arizona's voting rights struggles, documented in court filings, legislative testimony, and community archives, gains renewed urgency as a cultural resource requiring protection when the conditions that prompted those struggles begin to recur.

entries.T: Indiana State Law: FAIRNESS Act Requires Schools, Hospitals, and Local Governments to Cooperate with ICE Enforcement
entries.S: Signed into law by Governor Mike Braun. Some provisions effective immediately, others July 1, 2026. Community advocates met March 26 to discuss implications.
entries.D:
ACTION: Governor Mike Braun signed the FAIRNESS Act (SEA 76), requiring schools, hospitals, local governments, and law enforcement to comply with federal immigration enforcement. Businesses must verify employee legal status. Governmental bodies and public universities face fines up to $10,000 for knowingly restricting immigration enforcement. Community advocates met March 26 to discuss implications and plan responses.

EFFECTS: Mandating ICE cooperation from schools and hospitals transforms institutions of care and education into extensions of the immigration enforcement apparatus. Children in immigrant families may be kept home from school. Sick and injured individuals may avoid hospitals. The law creates a chilling effect on access to essential public services that extends far beyond undocumented individuals to affect entire mixed-status families and communities. The $10,000 fine provision penalizes local democratic decisions about community policing priorities. Indiana's significant immigrant communities, particularly in agricultural regions and cities like Indianapolis, face immediate and tangible harm.

immigrant.people: Immigrant communities in Indiana have built lives, businesses, families, and cultural institutions across the state over decades. The FAIRNESS Act transforms every interaction with public institutions into a potential immigration enforcement encounter by requiring schools, hospitals, and local governments to cooperate with ICE and imposing $10,000 fines on entities that restrict enforcement. A parent enrolling a child in school, a patient seeking emergency care, or a community member reporting a crime must weigh whether contact with any public institution could trigger deportation.
immigrant.places: Indiana's immigrant communities are concentrated in Indianapolis, Fort Wayne, Elkhart, South Bend, and agricultural communities. Churches conducting services in Spanish, Burmese, Karen, and other languages serve as community anchors. Ethnic grocery stores, restaurants, and cultural centers function as gathering places. The FAIRNESS Act transforms schools, hospitals, and government buildings from spaces of public service into potential enforcement sites.
immigrant.practices: Sending children to school, seeking medical care, calling police, attending public meetings, visiting libraries, and engaging with local government are civic practices the FAIRNESS Act disrupts. Immigrant families practice bilingual child-rearing, heritage language maintenance, and cultural transmission through religious observance, food preparation, and community celebration. All depend on the ability to interact with public institutions without fear.
immigrant.treasures: Community archives, cultural collections, religious objects, family photographs, and heritage language materials constitute irreplaceable records of migration and cultural adaptation. Community organizations' institutional records document decades of immigrant settlement. The relationships of trust between immigrant communities and public institutions, built over years, represent an intangible treasure the FAIRNESS Act deliberately destroys.

latine.people: Indiana's Latine community, estimated at over 500,000, includes families with roots spanning generations alongside recent arrivals. The FAIRNESS Act's ICE cooperation requirement falls disproportionately on this community as the primary targets of interior enforcement and racial profiling. U.S.-born Latine citizens report being asked for documentation, a humiliation the Act will intensify. Workers in agriculture, meatpacking, construction, and service industries face compounded vulnerability.
latine.places: Indianapolis's west side Latine neighborhoods, Elkhart's manufacturing communities, and agricultural towns represent cultural landscapes built through decades of investment. Catholic and Pentecostal churches with Spanish-language congregations, quinceañera venues, Latine-owned businesses, and cultural organizations like La Plaza all face reduced engagement when public presence becomes dangerous.
latine.practices: Quinceañera celebrations, Día de los Muertos observances, Mexican Independence Day festivals, Spanish-language religious services, ballet folklorico, mariachi music, and communal food traditions all depend on the ability to gather publicly without fear. Heritage language transmission is disrupted when families withdraw. Labor organizing becomes extremely dangerous when workplace complaints can trigger enforcement.
latine.treasures: Community murals, religious art, traditional textiles, musical instruments, and the material culture of decades of settlement are at risk. Community newspapers, radio programs, and social media networks function as living archives. Oral histories of Latine settlement in Indiana constitute historical records at risk when the community is driven into hiding.

children.people: Children in immigrant and mixed-status families face direct harm from the requirement that schools cooperate with ICE. Many are U.S. citizens by birth who depend on schools for meals, healthcare screenings, socialization, and stability. When parents fear school contact could trigger enforcement, children are kept home. The psychological impact includes anxiety, depression, difficulty concentrating, and post-traumatic stress.
children.places: Public schools, Head Start centers, after-school programs, libraries, parks, and recreation centers are primary places where children interact with public institutions. The Act transforms these from zones of safety into potential enforcement sites. Schools with high immigrant enrollment face enrollment declines. Pediatric clinics become dangerous when medical facilities must cooperate with ICE.
children.practices: Attending school, participating in bilingual education, accessing English Language Learner programs, and engaging in extracurricular activities are disrupted when schools become enforcement sites. Child welfare monitoring through which teachers identify children experiencing abuse or food insecurity is compromised when families avoid school contact.
children.treasures: Children's educational records, artwork, and developmental progress represent individual treasures disrupted by family separation or school withdrawal. Bilingual education materials and culturally responsive curricula represent institutional treasures. The relationships between educators and immigrant families are intangible treasures the enforcement mandate destroys.

mixedStatusFamilies.people: Mixed-status families, in which members hold different immigration statuses, represent one of the most common family structures in Indiana's immigrant communities. The FAIRNESS Act forces impossible calculations about which family members can safely interact with which institutions. A U.S.-citizen child may need a hospital but their undocumented parent cannot safely accompany them. These calculations fragment family life and force children into premature adult roles.
mixedStatusFamilies.places: Homes of mixed-status families become places of confinement when public spaces are dangerous. Family members with vulnerable status may be effectively homebound. Churches become fraught spaces when community members cannot be sure which spaces are surveilled.
mixedStatusFamilies.practices: Mixed-status families practice constant risk management shaping every aspect of daily life, from who drives the car to who answers the door. The FAIRNESS Act intensifies these practices to a degree making normal family functioning nearly impossible. Cultural practices requiring full family participation are disrupted when some members cannot safely leave home.
mixedStatusFamilies.treasures: The family unit itself is the primary treasure at risk. Family photographs, documents, and cultural objects recording history across borders are treasures heightened by the threat of separation. The knowledge parents transmit about navigating two cultures is an intangible treasure lost when families are separated or isolated.

allCommunities.people: All Indiana residents are affected when public institutions are conscripted into immigration enforcement because the trust between communities and institutions is a public good. When immigrants stop calling police, public safety decreases for all. When sick people avoid hospitals, communicable disease surveillance is compromised. When children are withdrawn from school, the educational environment is diminished for all students.
allCommunities.places: Public schools, hospitals, government offices, and community spaces are transformed from institutions of public service into arms of immigration enforcement. This affects the character and function of these spaces for all users.
allCommunities.practices: Public education, healthcare delivery, public safety, and local governance are all degraded when entangled with immigration enforcement. Professional practices refined over decades to serve diverse populations are distorted by the enforcement requirements.
allCommunities.treasures: The principle of separation between local public services and federal immigration enforcement, which has enabled effective service delivery regardless of immigration status, is a policy treasure. Research demonstrating the benefits of this separation constitutes a knowledge treasure. Community relationships enabling effective public service are social capital the FAIRNESS Act destroys.

entries.T: Ohio State Legislation: House Passes Drag Performance Ban (HB 249), Sponsors Say It Could Restrict Transgender People
entries.s: Ohio passes drag ban targeting trans people
entries.S: Passed Ohio House 63-32 (party line) on March 25, 2026. Moves to Ohio Senate.
entries.D:
ACTION: On March 25, 2026, the Ohio House passed HB 249, the 'Indecent Exposure Modernisation Act,' 63-32 on a party-line vote. The bill creates a new offense of 'unlawful adult cabaret performance,' restricts such performances to adults-only venues, and expands the definition of 'public indecency' from 'private parts' to 'private area.' Sponsors explicitly stated the bill could be used to restrict transgender people from using gendered facilities like locker rooms. Equality Ohio CEO called it an attempt to turn 'regular, everyday activities' into 'potential crimes.'

EFFECTS: The bill targets drag performers and, by its sponsors' own admission, transgender and gender non-conforming people. It criminalizes forms of cultural expression with deep roots in LGBTQ+ communities. Drag performance is a significant cultural art form with historical connections to Black, Latino, and queer communities. The expansion of 'public indecency' definitions to encompass broader physical characteristics creates a mechanism for criminalizing transgender existence in public spaces. The bill is part of a nationwide wave: the ACLU is tracking approximately 500 anti-LGBTQ bills across state legislatures in 2026.

lgbtq.people: LGBTQ communities understand drag as an art form rooted in queer cultural history, a tradition of creative self-expression that has served as a site of community building, resistance, and joy for over a century. Drag performers are artists whose work draws on costuming, makeup, choreography, comedy, and live performance. The bill's sponsors stated explicitly that HB 249 could restrict transgender people's access to gendered facilities, revealing the legislation's true scope. The legislation arrives in the context of approximately 500 anti-LGBTQ bills introduced nationally.
lgbtq.places: Gay bars, nightclubs, community centers, theaters, and pride festival venues in Ohio cities including Columbus, Cleveland, Cincinnati, Dayton, and Akron serve as the physical infrastructure of queer cultural life. Beyond dedicated LGBTQ spaces, drag performances occur at mainstream theaters, colleges, libraries, restaurants, and community events. HB 249's expansion of public indecency definitions threatens all of these venues by creating criminal liability.
lgbtq.practices: Drag performance encompasses lip-sync, live singing, comedy, dance, theatrical storytelling, and pageantry. Drag culture maintains its own mentorship systems and competitive structures. Drag brunches, bingo, storytelling hours, and charity events have become community institutions. The legislation also chills gender nonconformity more broadly, as its sponsors' statements reveal the bill targets not a specific act but a category of identity.
lgbtq.treasures: The material culture of drag, including costumes, wigs, makeup collections, and stage sets, represents significant artistic investment. Photographic and video archives documenting decades of drag performance constitute irreplaceable records of queer cultural history. The economic contributions of drag, including charitable funds and tourism revenue, represent community wealth. HB 249 threatens to drive this cultural production underground or out of state.

artists.people: The broader performing arts community, including actors, dancers, costume designers, makeup artists, choreographers, and venue operators, is affected by legislation criminalizing artistic expression based on gender presentation. Artists who work across genres recognize that expanding public indecency definitions to encompass gender-nonconforming performance creates a framework applicable to theater, dance, cabaret, and other art forms exploring gender and identity.
artists.places: Theaters, performance venues, galleries, and arts organizations across Ohio provide the performing arts infrastructure HB 249 threatens. Major institutions including Playhouse Square in Cleveland, the Ohio Theatre in Columbus, and the Aronoff Center in Cincinnati regularly present productions with gender-nonconforming performance. Smaller venues are even more vulnerable as they lack legal resources to navigate compliance uncertainty.
artists.practices: Theatrical performance, dance, comedy, cabaret, burlesque, and other live performance traditions all involve practices that could fall within HB 249's expanded definition of public indecency. Shakespeare productions featuring cross-dressing could technically be affected. Contemporary theater exploring gender fluidity faces direct threat. The bill's vagueness forces venues and artists toward self-censorship as the safest course.
artists.treasures: The collective body of theatrical, dance, comedic, and performance art created by Ohio artists working with themes of gender, identity, and transformation represents significant artistic value. Scripts, choreographic works, video recordings, and critical writing constitute an archive of creative achievement. The economic infrastructure of Ohio's performing arts sector represents community wealth that HB 249 threatens to diminish.

allCommunities.people: All Ohioans are affected by legislation expanding the state's power to define artistic expression as criminal conduct. The precedent extends beyond drag and LGBTQ communities to establish that the state can criminalize performance based on gender presentation, a standard with no clear boundary. The legislation reflects a broader climate of hostility toward gender nonconformity affecting everyone whose expression does not conform to rigid gender expectations.
allCommunities.places: Public spaces throughout Ohio, including parks where pride festivals are held, libraries that have hosted drag storytelling, community centers that have organized drag brunches, and municipal buildings where LGBTQ cultural events have occurred, all become sites of potential enforcement under HB 249.
allCommunities.practices: Free expression, artistic freedom, and the right to attend cultural events without fear of criminal prosecution are practices fundamental to democratic life that HB 249 threatens. The practice of community engagement through cultural programming is chilled when one category of performer is criminalized.
allCommunities.treasures: Ohio's reputation as a state with a vibrant cultural scene, its network of performance venues, and the economic activity generated by the performing arts represent collective treasures threatened by legislation driving artistic expression underground.

entries.n: Idaho HB 752 - Criminal Transgender Bathroom Ban
entries.T: Idaho State Legislation: House Passes Criminal Transgender Bathroom Ban with Felony Penalties Up to 5 Years
entries.s: Idaho criminalizes trans bathroom use with felony penalties
entries.S: Passed Idaho House March 16, 2026. Moves to Idaho Senate.
entries.D:
ACTION: On March 16, 2026, the Idaho House passed HB 752 creating criminal misdemeanor and felony charges for transgender people using bathrooms that do not match their sex assigned at birth. A first offense carries up to one year in prison (misdemeanor); a second offense within five years is a felony punishable by up to five years in prison. The bill applies to business and government buildings.

EFFECTS: This is one of the most extreme anti-transgender bills in the nation, imposing felony criminal penalties, including up to five years imprisonment, for the act of using a restroom. The criminalization of bathroom use effectively bars transgender people from participating in public life, as they cannot safely access any public restroom. The law creates a constant threat of arrest and prosecution for transgender Idahoans engaged in the most basic daily activities. The felony provision is particularly devastating: a felony conviction results in loss of voting rights, employment disqualification, housing barriers, and lifelong social stigma. This represents the escalation of anti-transgender legislation from restriction to criminalization.

lgbtq.people: Transgender and nonbinary Idahoans face criminal prosecution, including misdemeanor charges carrying up to one year and felony charges carrying up to five years, for using restrooms consistent with their gender identity. The criminal penalties are designed to force trans people out of public life entirely, since the inability to use a restroom effectively bars a person from workplaces, schools, commercial establishments, and government buildings. Trans youth face additional trauma of knowing their state has declared their existence criminal.
lgbtq.places: Every business and government building in Idaho becomes a site of potential criminal liability. Workplaces, schools, hospitals, retail stores, restaurants, government offices, libraries, and parks with restroom facilities are transformed into zones of legal danger. College campuses become particularly fraught. Healthcare facilities where trans people receive gender-affirming care become paradoxical spaces where medical transition is permitted but using a restroom is criminalized.
lgbtq.practices: The fundamental practice of living as a transgender person in public space is criminalized by HB 752. Trans people currently practice strategies for navigating gendered spaces, including using single-occupancy restrooms when available and restricting fluid intake.
